title: Dan Liubovich -Peer Interview | Chief of Staff - Ops & Special Projects
meeting_title: Dan Liubovich -Peer Interview | Chief of Staff - Ops & Special Projects

transcript.text: Hey, George.
Good.
How are you?
meeting is being recorded.
Let me know if you can you hear me all right?
Yeah.
Yeah, I can hear you.
Can you hear me?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I just took you off my headphone.
Just let me know if there's any background noise or anything.
But yeah, should be good.
For being a minute late, I just had a meeting run a little long.
Yeah.
No, no worries.
I totally understand.
Yeah, man.
Well, I was excited to meet you and glad we found the time.
Pretty much.
I mean, we can keep it like pretty casual.
then I have some questions that like I want to ask, but would definitely just learn more about you more generally.
Like who, you know, like who Dan is, like what you're interested in, kind of what you're doing out here in SF and your time at Zipline.
And then, yeah, like what drives you and excites you.
then we can get into, you know, like AI, like how to be more AI native.
And then like you're at more like direct experience on a project and stuff.
Totally.
But would love to know like more generally first.
Yeah.
Yeah, absolutely.
Happy to give some background.
So yeah, so Dan, great to meet you.
Been at Zip now for a little over a year.
And before that was at McKinsey for a little bit over four years.
For undergrad, I went to Georgia Tech, and I was actually in biomedical engineering, so very much not what I'm doing nowadays.

But yeah, so at McKinsey, I focused kind of on a lot of different things, but a lot of it was like operation strategy and kind of how you go from both establishing the actual strategy itself, but then creating the initiatives, working with the clients to actually both create the governance and actually do the implementation itself.
So I feel like I got a lot of good experience, both from like kind of the figuring out what to do and also understanding what actually goes into that from a tactical side of things.
And then since joining Zip, it's been kind of a lot of different things, you know, in the biz ops role here.
I'd say it's similar to a chief of staff in the sense that it's always like special projects and just kind
The of our customers up until that point, but certainly as we were moving up market, it kind of just broke down, essentially.
And also at the same time, like we were launching a bunch of new products in the last year or so.
And so the model just wasn't really set up for that.
So a lot of it was kind of, you know, at its core, okay, let's set up the actual like product infrastructure, essentially, of like, okay, what products need to be bundled together?
What's the underlying like, you know, I guess, for lack of a better term, like the like atom building blocks, if you will.

And then once we had that set up, it was more about figuring out like, okay, what pricing models are applicable, like in the market, what would make sense to our customers, and something that we can actually use for all these, all these products.

So ultimately, like that was the big goal was sort of like upgrading the pricing to be able to handle a lot more of the different nuance and situations, while also still keeping it simple enough where in like the, you know, 50% of times where the old one was still fine, we'd essentially be able to kind of do, you know, something that was very similar there.
Yeah.
And so.
But from that, a lot of it was like working with the sales team to understand like what are kind of the guardrails that we're operating within as well as like with the product team.
And then just sort of mapping like, okay, knowing, you know, this type of customer, this is kind of the price we're shooting for.
And, you know, here's the deals that we've closed historically with these size customers and essentially like mapping that to a pricing curve that we felt was appropriate.

And then from there, just a lot of iterating, testing with the sales teams, making sure that, again, we can, you know, make it not too complicated, but also be able to capture all the different nuances that we were trying to solve.
Yeah, nice.
Help me understand.
I'm just kind of curious.
So it sounds like there was maybe two parts.
It's like this, the product is good, but it was more general.
And so it started not to serve some of the customers that you began to target.
And then simultaneously, it sounds like you also had a few other products coming down on the roadmap that were getting ready for launch.
So like, to me, it sounds like there's a few things like...
You want more customers.
These customers look a little bit different.
How can we best serve them?
And how can we understand what they actually want?
Which is like when you talk to sales and like on the team.
And then the other piece, it sounds like, was we're doing all this other  too.
How can we figure out, like, was it figuring out like the best landing spot of the percentage of these existing customers?
Or just like, help me understand a little bit more of the specifics.
Yeah, no, it's a great question.

And I would say like, it was very much interconnected in the sense that like, the ultimate impetus was more of the first thing as far as like, hey, we're not going to just be selling this to like our standard ICP.
Like we're going to be going up market, down market, new verticals.
we need something that like, isn't just, hey, if it's, not that this was, but making up an example, like, hey, if it's 10,000 people, it's X dollars.
If it's 20,000 people, it's Y dollars.
Like we need something more robust than that.
Yeah.
then on top of that, it was very much like, okay, well, if we're also launching a bunch of new products, which we also just have to figure out the pricing for anyway, there's no sin.
It's in redesigning the pricing twice.
We may as well do it once and do it right.

So in that sense, there was sort of like, okay, historically, let's say, you know, some products that we used to have bundled that we now actually are going to separate out because they're actually different use cases.
There's a little bit of like, okay, let's look at the data.
Like, are customers even using this?
Like, does it make sense to separate out?
Would people pay for it?
On the flip side of it, was a bit more of like, okay, hey, we're launching a new contracting module, right?
So, like, what is typical for how those are priced in the market?

What would be, you know, we don't want to come in with a million-dollar price and, like, the norm for companies is between 100 and 200K or vice versa, come in at 10K when the norm is, you know, six figures, right?

So just sort of doing the calibration to make sure that, like, okay, whatever we're deciding for the new products is in line with what the market expects and still is going to fit with, like, the overall redesign that we decided to do to solve kind of the original problems.
And so it was a little bit of give and take, but I would say that was sort of the prioritization or at least, like, order of operations, if you will.
Yeah, yeah, that makes sense.
And then when you're talking about the contracting model that you just said, like, what did that research actually look like?

Like when you're trying to understand how to position the product and then how to price it, like help me understand like how the research came about, how you prioritize what you're looking at, competitors, et cetera.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, totally.
I think there was sort of, I guess, excuse me, two main elements to it that I would kind of bucket into like internal versus external.
So from an external perspective, it was a lot of like, you know, talking to some industry experts.
We had some like some of our marketing team that focuses specifically on like compete scenarios.

like talking to them about, you know, what they see externally as far as who the main competitors are in the market, what they typically price at, you know, just sort of more like benchmarking, if you will.
And then also talking to experts to understand like, okay, hey, what is normal in the market for contracts, which.
We basically found out you can price by number of contracts, you can price by the amount of spend that that covers, you can price by number of suppliers.
There's a bunch of different ways to do it.
But ultimately, we wanted something that was straightforward and could be applied everywhere.
And then from the internal perspective, a lot of it was more of the sales positioning of it.
So we had a lot of folks who, you know, maybe they came to zip from like an ironclad.
So they'd sold contract, you know, contracting software before, or maybe they'd worked, you know, in kind of a procurement role before and they, you know, had had to buy the software.
So they knew like, hey, if you come in with a 500k price tag, they're going to laugh you out of the room because they never pay that.
Right.

And so it was a little bit of like marrying kind of what I saw from like external research and talking to like sort of the internal, I don't know if experts is the right word, but like the internal folks who had been exposed to this before.
And ultimately, you know, just kind of aggregating all of that feedback between, you know, the 20 some odd folks that we had internally that could kind of speak to various parts of it.
And then from there, starting to like test a little bit with some, you know, core customers of ours who like.

Or on our advisory board, like, we knew we could trust and basically go to them and say, like, hey, if we were to go to you with this for a hypothetical new product module, like, does that make sense?
And I'd use that to, like, really pressure test, like, some of the objection handling and things like that.
So, and the testing was more, like, hey, how does this sound versus, hey, we have this thing, like, try it out and let me know, like, your feedback in terms of, like, a MVP on a product?

Yeah, yeah, it was very much, I would say, like, we knew that the product would work and we knew that the product was, like, set up in a way that, like, it had features that customers cared about, that customers would pay good money for.
It was more about how do we tell the story in a way that makes sense.
And we also, you know, kind of, like I was saying earlier, we didn't want it to get into something where, okay, well, we price our, you know, our P2P module on spend because that's normal.
And then we price our supplier module on number of suppliers because that's normal.
And then our contract module is on number of contracts and that's normal.
And, well, if you are.
So all three, then all of a sudden you're pricing on three different models and it's getting super confusing.
And, you know, even in that sense, like it wouldn't, it wouldn't make sense.
So it was more about finding something that like, hey, we can speak to this, you know, we know our contract module is going to be great.

We know that it's going to drive X amount of value, but what's a way that we can actually explain that and speak to people in a way that we can defend so that, you know, we can ultimately price at this and not have kind of the, you know, lose the forest in the trees, if that makes sense, once you're getting into a negotiation.
Yeah, yeah.
No, that does make sense.
And then what, what were the, like the specific outcomes?
Like were you focused just on that module?
Or yeah, like what, what, what came out of it?
Like what was the final, you know, outcome in terms of everything?
Absolutely.
So there were several outcomes and it really varied kind of by our customer segments.

So for the smaller segments, it was more about just like, hey, some of the things that we're doing or working, like no need to disrupt things, more of just, you know, adhering to the, the targets that some of our product teams already had as far.
It's like, hey, we want to sell, you know, we want X amount of customers on our contracting module after launch and so on.
So like their metrics, I guess, were like more normal, so to speak.

Whereas as we went upmarket, big part of what we were trying to do was break into, you know, again, more of the like global 2000 accounts, but also be able to really hone in on our land and expand motion as far as, you know, hey, we don't have to just sell like you buy everything, you get the whole zip suite.

And at the same time, like, you know, then maybe it's several million dollars and it seems like a huge price tag, even though like the value, you know, far outweighs the price, but being able to land as far as like, hey, we'll start with one project.
This is easier to approve.
It's a much faster sales cycle.
You can almost use it as like a proof of concept internally.
And so that was a big part of it as well.
Ultimately, like the final result, if you will, is it's now just used as our pricing model for all deals.
So pretty much all of our Q4 revenue, all of our Q3 revenue has been on this model.
And, you know, some of what I've been doing is just kind

Ironing out some of the kinks and working with sales on like feedback and other times it's, know, hey, we have kind of a weird situation where, you know, this is sort of an edge case where we have to kind of go off script from the model, right?
Like, Dan, help us brainstorm.
So it's been a lot of that as far as just, you know, closing deals especially, but ultimately, you know, overall trying to drive towards, hey, can we get more big customers, more of our new products?

There wasn't necessarily a specific like, hey, we need to see 20% increase in conversion rate by X date because it was very much like, hey, this is just the new pricing model for everyone going forward.
Yeah, no, that makes sense.
That makes sense.
That makes a ton of sense, like super helpful to add those specifics.

I guess it's more like, I mean, it's honestly more personal, like what about Zipline and those projects and, you know, it seemed like you had like a lot of visibility and you were able to make a big impact.
Like it had, it was like one of the biggest focuses of the company at the time, like restructuring how you price everything, basically how you're driving revenue, right?
Like what, what about you now, like wants to be closer to the smaller, scrappier, more ambiguity, like, like, yeah, I guess like what's driving you towards working on different things?
Like, it sounds like you've been pretty like successful there.
I think you're on mute, by the way.
Weird, I don't know what happened there.
No, it's a totally fair question.

I mean, I would say a big part of it, honestly, is like, you know, I, this pricing project, as an example, I really enjoy doing, I'm just, I'd say like good with numbers in general, and kind of like analytical exercises like this.
And, you know, fingers crossed, it's not something that we have to do again at Zip.
So in that sense, right, like going to an earlier stage startup means that there's just more of these types of problems that need to be solved.
Where, you know, with, with Zip, it was more of like, hey.
And we're noticing some problems and we want to get out ahead of them.

Whereas with you guys, it might be more of like, hey, how do we come up with a pricing model that's replicable and scalable in the first place based off of our existing customers and market research and so on?
So I would say, honestly, a big part of it for me is that there's just so many more problems to solve.
And I think that's ultimately what I want the most exposure to.
It's not really anything that's wrong with Zip per se.
It's almost, you know, I kind of, I'm kind of looking for Zip of two years ago, almost.
And for me, I feel like that's where I can like make the most impact.
And honestly, that's where I see the most similarity, like with you guys, as far as just where you are in, you know, your growth journey.
Yeah, totally get it here.
I'm letting someone in really quick.
Give me like 20 seconds, please.
Yeah, of course.
Sorry about that.
Thank you.
Sorry about that, man.
I'm usually in the office.
so the days that I'm from home, I stack, like, any maintenance tech that we need, any, like, I try to get it all done while I'm home.
So it makes it a little more busy, but, um, no, that's why I totally get it.
I mean, um, like I was in similar spots too.
So like, to be completely candid, like when people are looking for like a move, like a lot of good is happening.
There's also like a driver there of like, okay, like I want more, right.
I want more problems.
I want to be like challenged, want to do more.
And like, it sounds like you're kind of in that boat, which is exciting.
Cause like growth mindset, wanting to do more, wanting to solve hard problems.
was like,
A lot of people don't want that, right?
So it's hard to find people that do.
So that was really cool to hear.
guess I want to leave some room for questions at the end too, maybe like five for questions.
But for that, I would love to hear a little bit more.
You're doing a lot.
And it sounds like doing a lot of stuff that maybe you haven't done before for a while, which is like solving new problems.
It's hard.
A lot of research goes into it.
lot of thinking goes into it.

And I'm wondering in general, like at work and maybe in a specific process during work, like researching the strategy for the pricing model, like how are you, if and how, are you leveraging like all the new AI tools and what's possible now with research tools, just models in general and the way that you work?
Would love to understand like how you've been using it and how you're staying on top, how it's changing so far.
And the context there is I've never been at a place that's more AI native.
Ever than growthx.
it's hard because everything's changing, but like leading from the front means like Marcel and Daniel, our CTO and CEO, like they're always testing it too.
They're always trying to like become more efficient and no one really knows what's ahead.
But like what we do know is like we're seeing insane efficiency in everything that we do.
Like we just keep testing and seeing what the best approach is.
And it's like, it's very impressive, it's also like a weird new hard skill to like have or even be like genuinely interested in.
And so that's kind of the context of the question.
No, that's super helpful.

And honestly, that's great to hear because I would say like where I'm at, I would say is I feel like I know enough AI to like be dangerous, but I really want to get to that point where I feel like I'm, you know, AI native, like you said, because I, you know, I feel like I see other people who are able to do that.
And like for them, it's like, you know, maybe I'm able to like two or three X my productivity and they can 10 X their productivity.
And, you know, frankly, it's crazy.
So that's great to hear.
That's honestly, I want to learn exactly that.
To answer your question more directly, I couldn't have done it, I think, without AI at all.

I think from the simplest standpoint of just like, hey, can I do research in a way that instead of me spending multiple days reading all sorts of articles and studies and things like that, like asking Gemini to run a deep research report or perplexity to provide certain answers.
And obviously, because you're able to also like dive into the sources and make sure that like, okay, it didn't just make up some BS, like it actually, you know, did properly research this.
So I feel like that at the very least was like, hey, like, let me take a first principles pass on my own.
And let me also ask AI and then kind of compare notes at the end to see where I was off versus not, and sort of use that to like help my brainstorming.

And then, you know, very tactically, right, like I built our entire interactive pricing calculator, it's like a internally deployed web app that we host in Okta, but I built the entire thing by coding.

I'm using Gemini and Cloud for the most part, not even like in the IDE, really just like in the chat window, because it ultimately started where we had a calculator in Excel, and it was just kind of clunky and getting too complicated.
And I literally just asked AI, think I was like, hey, like, help me make like an interactive dashboard of this that's like easier to explain.
And once it like created the first pass of it, I was like, wait a minute, like, if I can do that, I can just build in all the, I just built into Excel, and then I can just build it there.
And, you know, everybody's been, I've had a bunch of sales reps be like, this is the most, you know, useful and impressive pricing calculator I've ever used.
It's better than our CPQ tool, frankly.

So like, internally, we're thinking through like, hey, how can we like do the outputs from the pricing calculator so that I can automate like SoW creation, and like automate like our Salesforce fields, because frankly, the calculator is better than whatever the reps are using now.
And yeah, all of that was because of AI, like, you know, I coded back in the day, you know, in undergrad, but I would say like, my coding experience after McKinsey was very much.

Our clients and organizing and bucketing our clients in different categories, not just based on demographics, but actually things that matter in terms of product and how we're positioning and what customers we want to scale and expand.
Because $100,000 from two customers isn't the same $100,000.
One's easier to serve.
One is more impactful.
One is easier for our team to deliver on.
And so coming out of that, we gain this understanding of who our ICP is and why down to like five different matrices.
And I'm like, okay, this is good.
This is good information.
But like, how can we make it easier to qualify every new client that we have in like a scoring system?
so similar to you, built out like a tool that sales can use that would score based on all these different criteria within each matrix.
So at the end, it's like we can now have an objective conversation about why this client is better.
And like, we're not just talking in our pipeline meetings about or solely relying on our...
Salesperson would, like, anecdotal feedback and, like, why he thinks someone's better.
It's like, this is the score.
This is where the problem areas, the red flags that we've noticed.
Let's talk through theories, unblock, either qualify or disqualify, and move on.
And so that was, like, a big thing do.
so, like, that is a tool that's easy for sales to, like, go and create that ICP score from.
And, like, that's, like, another simple example of how can we just work better and, like, faster and more effective as well.
Totally.
Totally on the same page there.
Yeah, honestly, that's an interesting example you bring up, honestly, though, because, you know, think back to, you know, your earlier question as far as, like, why leave Zip?
And I feel like a lot of, like, the problems that I might solve at Zip is, like, hey, we've been doing this thing and we realize it's wrong and we need to fix it.
Whereas, kind of, like, you're talking about, like, you're able to come up with the basis for it in the first place.
And I love, like, what you were just talking about, right?
Because, like, okay, well, based off of this scorecard that we just created and automated, it's scalable because it's easy to expand this.
It's objective because we have, like, specific rules in place.
It's also something that we can change because if we change the underlying parameters, it'll just update the scoring.
And if we go into a new vertical, we can say like, hey, for this vertical, whatever, this part of the workflow is significantly more important and change the weighting.
And in that sense, you can not only create kind of the initial process, but you can also make it so that it's just scalable in the first place.
So then you don't have to go through like the messy rework down the road.

So I definitely, you know, that's part of what, you know, really excites me is to kind of, you solve those problems and hopefully solve them correctly the first time so that it doesn't end up being something that, you know, requires iteration down the road.
Yeah.
Yeah.
The thing is, man, there's no lack of problems here for sure.
Any company that's growing this fast, like it's more about the prioritization of what you're working on and why.
Totally.
using the problem versus there's always going to be everyone's problem to them is going to be the most important problem to solve.
I'm going to say that as like chief of staff, you need to like ingest, right.
Understand like what's more, more.
It's why, when to like actually focus your time on a problem, when to loop in like Marcel, like how to best use his time.
And so, yeah, it's a lot, but it's good.
It's like definitely super energizing.
Cool, man.
I mean, I think we have like three minutes left here.
I don't have like a super hard stop, but happy to answer any questions if you have.
Yeah.
Yeah, absolutely.
Absolutely.
I mean, I think that's actually a great segue of what you were bringing up.
I'm very curious about kind of like what you see as like the top, you know, like how you prioritize for Marcel, like what are some of the biggest things on his mind?
Like, you know, I think to use Zip as an example, let's say like our CEO is very, very much involved in deals, right?
Versus like on the product side of things, you obviously knows it well, but like that's what the other co-founder, our CTO is for.
So in that sense, like if there was ever like a deal at risk, like to me, that's like, okay, I know to elevate this to our CEO.
Yo.
Versus trying to solve it myself.

So I'm more just curious, like, what are sort of the things that, you know, Marcel really cares about that help you say, like, okay, this is an immediate priority for him versus, you know, not so much?
Yeah.
Yeah, I'd say it was, like, so early when I was very, like, when I was more doing chief of staff stuff, did it?
Like, the company was more early.
And so, like, the problems that Marcel was working on were just also the problems that I was working on.
Because, we'd both be in the office and, like, we're both trying to work on, like, the hardest stuff.
And so it was just, like, a natural we're both working on the same things.
But to your question, like, Marcel's job and, like, where he naturally excels is, like, vision, driving the story, the positioning forward.
Especially now where, like, things change so much.
AI visibility is changing so much.
Workflows, AI content in general, like, the marketing space is changing so much.
And so, like, where he really thrives is, like, when he...

It comes back in on occasion every week, every two weeks and like resets or reframes like a small thing that we've been doing because he's an expert who's lived in the space for 25 years and it's his baby, right?
So vision, sales, he's like one of the best natural salespeople I've ever met.
And so any, he still comes in and closes most of our new deals and any like at-risk account that is pretty big or pretty nuanced will work through internally.
With like the account holder and then our head of customer ops.
And then if it's something that like, hey, it's just a relationship thing, right?
Like Marcel knows him or it's more CEO to CEO talk outside of like, you know, the account driver and then their POC at the company.
Something just need and can be solved much quicker with escalation.
So it's case by case, but we still loop him in on accounts like that.
Um, and then right now he still spends a ton of time on, again, it's division and the.
But across all of our lanes.
So like we'll come in and rebuild different parts of the business, right?
And like set the bar for excellence and then staff it accordingly.
So an example is our onboarding process.
We didn't have one when I first joined.
And so Marcel and I jumped in and stood up our eight week strategy sprint, which is an accelerated onboarding to understand who we're serving, set them up essentially in our product perfectly.
So we get like the right amount of outputs and get enough early signals where they're confident in a longer term contract.
And so that is like an eight week process that we've now through, I think 70 reps, every new client goes through.
And now we've iterated on and now the team is like 15 people and we're constantly improving.
He did the same thing on the product side.
Our CTO is amazing and he manages the entire product team, but there's still Marcel has a level of extent understanding because he's building the product that he would have wanted.
In his 20 years as CMO.
so on less on like the actual.

Actual technical capability side and more on who the actual user is and what matters in the product and what to prioritize, that's where Marcel still spends a lot of time too, helping drive the team and prioritize because things are changing so quick and you can ship new features and whatnot so quick and competitors moving so fast as well.
So he still is involved in all the key areas of the business.
It's more of like what level of abstraction is he at and making sure, you know, like all the other stuff that everyone's going to worry about.
Most of the 80% can be solved before it even needs, you know, Marcel's time basically.
And so it's kind of understanding we're figuring out what that looks like.
I hope that that was kind of a long-winded answer, but I hope that answered the question.
No, that definitely helps.
And I mean, it makes sense, right?
Like he's, he's the one who like is his company, his vision.
He's the one who had all the experience.
you know, just like you said, right?
Like he's trying to solve the problems that he wish he, he could have had solved.
He was in that role.
So pulling him in from, like you said, from a positioning or strategy perspective just makes a ton of sense to be able to recalibrate that way.

And then everything else is, you know, I would think are kind of traditional, like the CEO's things that he's going to care about the most are going to be like your top customers, your top deals, like that.
You know, that all kind of makes sense.
So, yeah, that was super, super helpful.
I know we're at time here.
I guess just, you know, last quick question that I like to ask everybody.
Buddy, what's your favorite part at working at GrowthX and I guess just general thoughts on the culture there?
Yeah, yeah.
I'm a little weird about it.
Like, I think I'm a little weird about work in general.
Like, I chose it and like I chose where I'm at and like I worked really hard to like find a company where I can have a ton of impact and where I know the people beforehand.
So, like, I really love it.
I think it's a place where like whatever you want to do, it's possible in terms of growth or in terms of like what you're working on.
Like, we're still so lean and nimble and the way we operate is more like.
Like, let's get to the truth.
Let's think first principle.
What's the best answer?
So that removes any, like, hierarchy, right?
And so whatever you want is there for you in terms of what you want to work on, how much impact you want to have, and how much you want to work, right?
And so that part I really love about the culture.
And I would say I've never been at a place where I'm more impressed at the people that I'm working or more impressed by the people that I'm working with.

Marcel and Daniel specifically, our CEO and CTO, both, like, as experts as you can be in their domains, but still humble enough to know that we're in this crazy time and, like, let's, pardon my language, like, let's just  sprint to figure this out.
Yeah.
And let's know that, like, anything new that comes out, we have to test and see because no one's done this before or been here before.
And so.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Peer interview with Dan Liubovich for the Chief of Staff role.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=21.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Zipline Pricing Project:** Dan led a company-wide pricing overhaul to support upmarket expansion and new product launches. The new model is now the standard for all deals, enabling a land-and-expand motion.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
  - [**AI-Native Productivity:** Dan built a critical internal pricing calculator using AI (Gemini, Claude) with no prior web dev experience, demonstrating a strong ability to leverage new tools for rapid, high-impact results.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1248.0)
  - [**Motivation for GrowthX:** Seeks the scrappier, ambiguous environment of an earlier-stage startup to solve foundational problems from scratch, a contrast to Zipline's current focus on refining existing systems.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=907.0)
  - [**GrowthX CEO's Focus:** Marcel (CEO) focuses on vision, sales (closing major deals, saving at-risk accounts), and setting the bar for excellence in key functions like client onboarding.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1591.0)

## Topics

### Background & Zipline Experience

  - [**Experience:** 4+ years at McKinsey (ops strategy) → 1+ year at Zipline (Biz Ops).](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=63.0)
  - [**Zipline Role:** Biz Ops (similar to Chief of Staff), focused on special projects.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=109.0)
  - [**Key Project: Pricing Model Overhaul**](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
      - [**Problem:** Zipline's simple pricing model (e.g., per-user) failed to support upmarket expansion and new product launches.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
      - [**Goal:** Create a robust, flexible model to handle market nuance while remaining simple for standard deals.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=330.0)
      - [**Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=541.0)
          - [**Internal:** Collaborated with Sales and Product to define guardrails and map pricing curves to historical deal data.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
          - [**External:** Researched competitor pricing and market norms (e.g., for a new contracting module).](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=541.0)
          - [**Validation:** Pressure-tested concepts with advisory board customers to refine objection handling.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=654.0)
      - [**Outcome:** The new model is the standard for all deals (Q3/Q4 revenue), enabling a land-and-expand motion for large accounts.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=750.0)

### AI-Native Productivity

  - [**Dan's AI Use:**](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1107.0)
      - [**Research:** Used Gemini and Perplexity for rapid, first-principles research, cross-referencing AI outputs with manual findings.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1204.0)
      - [**Development:** Built an interactive web-based pricing calculator using AI (Gemini, Claude) in a chat window, with no prior web dev experience.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1248.0)
          - [**Impact:** The tool is more effective than the company's CPQ and is being explored for automating SoW creation and Salesforce updates.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1288.0)
  - [**George's AI Use (GrowthX):**](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1380.0)
      - [Built an ICP scoring tool for sales to objectively qualify leads based on 5+ matrices, replacing anecdotal feedback.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1380.0)
      - [**Impact:** Enables objective pipeline discussions and scalable, adaptable qualification criteria.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1415.0)

### Motivation for GrowthX

  - [**Core Driver:** Seeks more foundational, ambiguous problems to solve from scratch.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=907.0)
  - [**Contrast:** Zipline's current focus is on refining existing systems, whereas Dan wants to build them.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1470.0)
  - [**Analogy:** Seeks a company at Zipline's stage \~2 years ago, where he can have maximum impact.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=985.0)

### GrowthX Culture & CEO's Focus

  - [**Culture:**](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1886.0)
      - [High-impact environment with no hierarchy; decisions are based on first principles.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1915.0)
      - [Inspiring team, especially the CEO (Marcel) and CTO (Daniel), who are domain experts but humble and AI-native.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1940.0)
      - [Demanding work, as the team is building in a new space.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=2007.0)
  - [**Marcel (CEO) Focus:**](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1591.0)
      - [**Vision & Positioning:** Drives the company's narrative in the rapidly changing AI market.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1654.0)
      - [**Sales:** Closes most new deals and handles at-risk accounts, leveraging his 25+ years of experience.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1691.0)
      - [**Setting Standards:** Builds and scales key functions (e.g., the 8-week onboarding sprint) before staffing them.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1742.0)
      - [**Product Strategy:** Guides product prioritization based on user needs, not technical details.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=1781.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**George:** Follow up with Dan on next steps.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=2021.0)
  - [**Dan:** Send any additional questions to George.](https://fathom.video/share/2r613urz1R2oatRuJgxkUy54iE47zPxf?tab=summary&timestamp=2021.0)
